schools.summary: The angel of the LORD coming up from Gilgal to Bochim, declaring God's faithful covenant against Israel's unfaithfulness: 'and an angel of the LORD came up from Gilgal to Bochim, and said, I made you to go up out of Egypt, and have brought you unto the land which I sware unto your fathers; and I said, I will never break my covenant with you' — the faithful covenant — 'and an angel of the LORD came up from Gilgal to Bochim, and said, I made you to go up out of Egypt, and have brought you unto the land which I sware unto your fathers; and I said, I will never break my covenant with you': the angel, speaking in God's name, rehearses his mercies and his unbroken faithfulness (the witnesses noting the significance — the LORD setting his own perfect faithfulness against Israel's unfaithfulness, reminding them that he had kept every word of the covenant, brought them out of Egypt and into the land, and never broken his part, so that the blame for the breach lay wholly with them, the angel, by some held the Captain of the host or a prophet in God's name, by many the divine Angel of the covenant, coming from Gilgal, the place of the first encampment and circumcision, to Bochim, 'I will never break my covenant,' the lesson — that God is faithful to his covenant though his people are faithless, the breach never on his side, 'if we believe not, yet he abideth faithful: he cannot deny himself,' 2 Timothy 2:13, the truth that God's mercies bind his people to obedience, and his faithfulness aggravates their sin, the covenant kept by him and broken by them, 'I am the LORD, I change not,' Malachi 3:6, the figure — the Angel of the covenant, the LORD himself pleading with his people, fulfilled in Christ the Mediator who keeps the covenant on God's part, 'a faithful high priest,' the truth that God's unbreakable faithfulness is the ground of all hope and the measure of all guilt, the figure of the unbreakable covenant of God set against the broken faith of his people, the call to answer God's faithfulness with obedience, the warning that to break faith with so faithful a God is the deepest sin, the figure of the unbreakable covenant of God, set against the broken faith of his people); the angel of the LORD coming from Gilgal to Bochim and rehearsing God's mercies, the deliverance from Egypt and the gift of the land sworn to the fathers (the faithful covenant, Malachi 3:6) — the message (I brought you up); 'and I said, I will never break my covenant with you' (God declaring he had never broken his covenant, his perfect faithfulness set against Israel's unfaithfulness, the breach wholly theirs, fulfilled in Christ the Angel of the covenant who keeps it on God's part, the faithfulness of God, 2 Timothy 2:13) — the figure (the Angel of the covenant pleading with his people, the providence, the message, the charge of their disobedience to follow, vv.2) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the angel's word 'I will never break my covenant' establish?

sides.position: The unbreakable covenant of God set against the broken faith of his people - the LORD setting his perfect faithfulness against Israel's unfaithfulness, having kept every word while they broke theirs, the breach wholly theirs, God faithful though his people are faithless (2 Timothy 2:13) and unchanging (Malachi 3:6), a figure of Christ the Angel and Mediator of the covenant

the angel of the LORD at Bochim declaring God's faithful covenant against Israel's unfaithfulness (the crux of the unbreakable covenant of God set against the broken faith of his people)
the LORD setting his perfect faithfulness against Israel's unfaithfulness, having kept every word and never broken his part, the blame for the breach wholly theirs
God faithful though his people are faithless, a figure of Christ the Angel and Mediator of the covenant who keeps it on God's part (2 Timothy 2:13; Malachi 3:6)

schools.summary: The charge that Israel had not obeyed God's voice: 'and ye shall make no league with the inhabitants of this land; ye shall throw down their altars: but ye have not obeyed my voice: why have ye done this?' — God had commanded them to make no league and throw down the altars, but they disobeyed (the and ye shall make no league with the inhabitants of this land; ye shall throw down their altars, the command, the but ye have not obeyed my voice: why have ye done this?, the disobedience charged, the figure, God's command broken, the league made and the altars spared, the truth that Israel disobeyed the express command to spare neither the people nor their idols, Exodus 34:12-13, the figure of the disobedience that spares what God condemns, the league and the altars, the charge of disobedience, the broken command, vv.1); the command to make no league and throw down the altars (the command, Exodus 34:12-13) — the command (no league); 'ye have not obeyed my voice: why have ye done this?' (the disobedience charged, the rebuke, the figure, the disobedience that spares what God condemns, the providence, the charge, the consequence that the nations would remain to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The consequence, the nations left as thorns and their gods a snare: 'wherefore I also said, I will not drive them out from before you; but they shall be as thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare unto you' — because of their disobedience, God would leave the nations to be thorns and their idols a snare (the wherefore I also said, I will not drive them out from before you, the nations left, the but they shall be as thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare unto you, thorns and a snare, the figure, the foe left as thorns, the idols a snare, the just judgment of disobedience, the truth that the spared nations became the punishment of the sin that spared them, Numbers 33:55, the figure of the tolerated sin that becomes a scourge, the foe a thorn, the idols a snare, the consequence of disobedience, the thorns and the snare, vv.2); God leaving the nations because of their disobedience (the judgment) — the judgment (not driven out); 'they shall be as thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare unto you' (the nations a thorn and the idols a snare, the scourge, Numbers 33:55, the figure, the tolerated sin that becomes a scourge, the providence, the judgment, the people weeping at Bochim to follow, vv.4-5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The place named Bochim, where they sacrificed: 'and they called the name of that place Bochim: and they sacrificed there unto the LORD' — the place was named Bochim, the place of weepers, and they offered sacrifice (the and they called the name of that place Bochim, named Bochim, the and they sacrificed there unto the LORD, the sacrifice, the figure, the place of weeping marked by a name and a sacrifice, the sorrow owned before God, the truth that the place was named for their weeping and sacrifice offered, the conviction acknowledged, the figure of the sorrow owned before God in worship, the tears and the sacrifice, the name Bochim, the place of weepers, vv.4); the place named Bochim, the weepers (the name) — the name (Bochim); 'they sacrificed there unto the LORD' (the sacrifice offered, the worship, the figure, the sorrow owned before God in worship, the providence, the sacrifice, the rehearsal of Joshua's dismissal to follow, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Joshua dismissing the people to possess the land: 'and when Joshua had let the people go, the children of Israel went every man unto his inheritance to possess the land' — the writer rehearses how Joshua had sent the people to their inheritances (the and when Joshua had let the people go, Joshua dismissed them, the the children of Israel went every man unto his inheritance to possess the land, each to his inheritance, the figure, the rehearsal of the good beginning, the people sent to possess the land, before the decline, the truth that the people went to their inheritances under Joshua, the faithful age, Joshua 24:28, the figure of the good beginning that the following decline would mar, the inheritance to be possessed, the dismissal of the people, the possessing of the land, vv.5); Joshua dismissing the people (the dismissal, Joshua 24:28) — the dismissal (Joshua let them go); 'the children of Israel went every man unto his inheritance to possess the land' (each to his inheritance, the possessing, the figure, the good beginning before the decline, the providence, the dismissal, Israel serving the LORD in Joshua's days to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Israel serving the LORD all the days of Joshua and the elders: 'and the people served the LORD all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders that outlived Joshua, who had seen all the great works of the LORD, that he did for Israel' — Israel served God while Joshua and the elders who had seen God's works lived (the and the people served the LORD all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders that outlived Joshua, Israel served while they lived, the who had seen all the great works of the LORD, that he did for Israel, the eyewitnesses, the figure, the faithfulness of the age while the eyewitnesses lived, the influence of godly leaders, the truth that the people served God while those who had seen his works remained, Joshua 24:31, the figure of the influence of godly leaders and the eyewitnesses of God's works, the next generation at risk, the faithfulness of the age, the influence of the elders, vv.6); Israel serving God while Joshua and the elders lived (the faithfulness, Joshua 24:31) — the faithfulness (served the LORD); 'who had seen all the great works of the LORD, that he did for Israel' (the elders who had seen God's works, the eyewitnesses, the figure, the influence of godly leaders, the providence, the faithfulness, the death of Joshua to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That when the eyewitness generation died, another arose that knew not the LORD nor his works: 'and also all that generation were gathered unto their fathers: and there arose another generation after them, which knew not the LORD, nor yet the works which he had done for Israel' — the generation that forgot God — 'and also all that generation were gathered unto their fathers: and there arose another generation after them, which knew not the LORD, nor yet the works which he had done for Israel': when the generation that had seen God's works died, a new one arose ignorant of him (the witnesses noting the significance — the swift and tragic decline of a people who failed to transmit the knowledge of God to their children, the eyewitnesses passing and the next generation knowing neither the LORD nor his mighty works, the root of all the apostasy that follows, the failure of the fathers to teach, and of the children to learn, the knowledge of God not handed down, 'they knew not the LORD,' the works forgotten that should have been told, the lesson — that the knowledge of God is not inherited but must be taught and learned in each generation, and that where it is not handed down, apostasy swiftly follows, 'tell ye your children of it, and let your children tell their children,' Joel 1:3, the truth that the fathers are charged to teach their children the works of God, lest the generation to come forget him, 'that the generation to come might know them... who should arise and declare them to their children: that they might set their hope in God, and not forget the works of God,' Psalm 78:6-7, the figure — the peril of every age that does not teach its children the faith, the candle of the knowledge of God going out in a single generation's neglect, the truth that not to know the LORD nor his works is the seed of all idolatry and ruin, the figure of the failure to hand down the knowledge of God and the apostasy that follows, the call to teach the next generation the works of God diligently, the warning that a generation that forgets God's works falls into apostasy, the figure of the failure to hand down the knowledge of God, and the apostasy that follows a generation's forgetting); the generation that had seen God's works dying and gathered to their fathers, the eyewitnesses passing away (the decline begun, Psalm 78:6-7) — the passing (gathered to their fathers); 'and there arose another generation after them, which knew not the LORD, nor yet the works which he had done for Israel' (a new generation arising that knew neither the LORD nor his works, the knowledge of God not handed down, the root of the apostasy to follow, the fathers failing to teach and the children to learn, the knowledge not transmitted, Joel 1:3) — the figure (the candle of the knowledge of God going out in a single generation's neglect, the providence, the decline, the apostasy and idolatry to follow, vv.11-13) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the rise of a generation that knew not the LORD signify?

sides.position: The failure to hand down the knowledge of God and the apostasy that follows a generation's forgetting - the eyewitnesses passing and the next generation ignorant of him and his works, the root of all the apostasy that follows, the knowledge of God not inherited but to be taught in each generation (Joel 1:3), the fathers charged to teach lest the generation to come forget (Psalm 78:6-7)

schools.summary: That Israel forsook the LORD their deliverer and followed the gods of the nations around them: 'and they forsook the LORD God of their fathers, which brought them out of the land of Egypt, and followed other gods, of the gods of the people that were round about them, and bowed themselves unto them, and provoked the LORD to anger' — the forsaking of God — 'and they forsook the LORD God of their fathers, which brought them out of the land of Egypt, and followed other gods, of the gods of the people that were round about them, and bowed themselves unto them, and provoked the LORD to anger': Israel abandoned the God who had redeemed them and turned to the idols of the surrounding nations (the witnesses noting the significance — the depth and ingratitude of the apostasy, Israel forsaking the very God who had brought them out of Egypt, the covenant God of their fathers, to bow down to the dead gods of the heathen around them, the basest exchange and the provoking of God's anger, the LORD their deliverer forsaken, the gods of the nations followed, the worship due to God given to idols, 'they forsook the fountain of living waters, and hewed them out cisterns, broken cisterns, that can hold no water,' Jeremiah 2:13, the lesson — that to forsake the living God for idols is the height of folly and ingratitude, the heart that leaves its Maker for the creature, the basest treason against the God of mercies, 'my people have changed their glory for that which doth not profit,' Jeremiah 2:11, the truth that apostasy provokes the just anger of God, who is jealous for his honour and will not give his glory to another, 'they provoked him to jealousy with strange gods,' Deuteronomy 32:16, the figure — the soul that forsakes God for the idols of the world, the spiritual adultery that leaves the husband for strangers, the heart's affections given to the creature, the truth that the God who redeems claims the whole heart, and to give it to idols is to provoke his anger, the figure of the great apostasy, the heart's turning from the living God to the idols of the world, the call to cleave to the God who redeemed us and give him our whole heart, the warning that forsaking God for idols provokes his just anger, the figure of the great apostasy, the heart's turning from the living God to the idols of the world); Israel forsaking the LORD, the covenant God of their fathers who had redeemed them from Egypt, the deepest ingratitude (the apostasy, Jeremiah 2:13) — the forsaking (the LORD their deliverer); 'and followed other gods, of the gods of the people that were round about them, and bowed themselves unto them, and provoked the LORD to anger' (Israel following the idols of the surrounding nations and bowing to them, the basest exchange that provoked God's just anger, who will not give his glory to another, the provoking to jealousy, Jeremiah 2:11; Deuteronomy 32:16) — the figure (the spiritual adultery that leaves the husband for strangers, the providence, the apostasy, God's anger and the spoilers to follow, vv.14) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Israel's forsaking the LORD for other gods signify?

sides.position: The great apostasy, the heart's turning from the living God to the idols of the world - the depth and ingratitude of forsaking the God who redeemed them to bow to the dead gods of the heathen, the basest exchange (Jeremiah 2:13) and changing their glory for what does not profit (Jeremiah 2:11), provoking the just anger of God who will not give his glory to another (Deuteronomy 32:16)

schools.summary: God's anger and his delivering Israel to spoilers: 'and the anger of the LORD was hot against Israel, and he delivered them into the hands of spoilers that spoiled them, and he sold them into the hands of their enemies round about, so that they could not any longer stand before their enemies' — God's anger burned, and he gave Israel into the hands of spoilers (the and the anger of the LORD was hot against Israel, and he delivered them into the hands of spoilers that spoiled them, delivered to spoilers, the and he sold them into the hands of their enemies round about, so that they could not any longer stand before their enemies, sold to their enemies, the figure, God's just anger giving the apostate people into the hands of their foes, the judgment of their sin, the truth that God sold them to their enemies, the protection withdrawn, Deuteronomy 32:30, the figure of the judgment that follows apostasy, the people given to the spoiler, no longer able to stand, the anger of the LORD, the deliverance to spoilers, vv.13); God's anger delivering Israel to spoilers (the judgment, Deuteronomy 32:30) — the anger (delivered to spoilers); 'he sold them into the hands of their enemies round about' (God selling them to their foes, the protection withdrawn, the judgment, the figure, the judgment that follows apostasy, the providence, the judgment, the distress whithersoever they went to follow, vv.15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That, despite Israel's sin, the LORD raised up judges to deliver them: 'nevertheless the LORD raised up judges, which delivered them out of the hand of those that spoiled them' — the sovereign mercy — 'nevertheless the LORD raised up judges, which delivered them out of the hand of those that spoiled them': when Israel was justly given to the spoiler, God in mercy raised up deliverers (the witnesses noting the significance — the free and unmerited mercy of God, who, though justly angry, did not utterly forsake his people, but raised up judges to save them from their oppressors, the 'nevertheless' of grace breaking in upon the judgment they had earned, the deliverers raised up not by Israel's deserving but by God's pity, the judges his gift to a people who had forsaken him, the lesson — that God's mercy triumphs over judgment, sparing and saving the undeserving, the deliverer sent in the depth of their misery, 'where sin abounded, grace did much more abound,' Romans 5:20, the truth that salvation is wholly of God's raising up, the deliverer his gift, not earned by the delivered, 'the LORD raised up... a deliverer,' the figure — the judges a type of Christ the great Deliverer, raised up by God to save his people from their enemies, 'he raised up an horn of salvation for us,' Luke 1:69, the truth that every deliverance of God's people flows from his sovereign mercy and not their merit, the Saviour sent to the lost, the figure of the sovereign mercy that sends deliverers to the undeserving, the call to own that our deliverance is wholly of God's grace, the assurance that God does not forsake his people utterly but raises up salvation, the figure of the sovereign mercy of God, who sends deliverers to his undeserving people); God, despite his just anger, raising up judges to deliver his people, the 'nevertheless' of mercy breaking in upon judgment (the sovereign mercy, Romans 5:20) — the raising up (the LORD raised up judges); 'which delivered them out of the hand of those that spoiled them' (the judges delivering Israel from their spoilers, the deliverer God's free gift to an undeserving people, a type of Christ the great Deliverer raised up to save, the deliverer raised up, Luke 1:69) — the figure (the judges a type of Christ the great Deliverer, the providence, the deliverance, the people's continued unfaithfulness to follow, vv.17) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the LORD raising up judges signify?

sides.position: The sovereign mercy of God, who sends deliverers to his undeserving people - the free and unmerited mercy that, though justly angry, did not utterly forsake his people but raised up deliverers, the 'nevertheless' of grace breaking in upon judgment (Romans 5:20), salvation wholly of God's raising up, the judges a type of Christ the horn of salvation (Luke 1:69)

themes: Nevertheless the LORD raised up judges, which delivered them out of the hand of those that spoiled them
that, despite Israel's sin, the LORD raised up judges to deliver them (the crux of the sovereign mercy of God, who sends deliverers to his undeserving people)
the free and unmerited mercy of God, who though justly angry did not utterly forsake his people but raised up deliverers, the 'nevertheless' of grace breaking in upon judgment
salvation wholly of God's raising up, the judges a type of Christ the horn of salvation raised up to save (Romans 5:20; Luke 1:69)

schools.summary: That Israel would not hearken to their judges but went whoring after other gods: 'and yet they would not hearken unto their judges, but they went a whoring after other gods, and bowed themselves unto them: they turned quickly out of the way which their fathers walked in, obeying the commandments of the LORD; but they did not so' — Israel would not obey the judges but turned again to idols (the and yet they would not hearken unto their judges, but they went a whoring after other gods, and bowed themselves unto them, would not hearken, went whoring after idols, the they turned quickly out of the way which their fathers walked in, obeying the commandments of the LORD; but they did not so, turned quickly aside, the figure, the obstinate unfaithfulness that would not heed the judges, the spiritual whoredom, the truth that Israel turned quickly from the way of their godly fathers, the heart unfaithful, Exodus 34:15, the figure of the spiritual adultery that forsakes God for idols even under the deliverer, the heart unfaithful, the unfaithfulness, the turning aside, vv.16); Israel not hearkening but going whoring after idols (the unfaithfulness, Exodus 34:15) — the unfaithfulness (whoring after gods); 'they turned quickly out of the way which their fathers walked in' (the quick turning from the way of the fathers, the apostasy, the figure, the spiritual adultery that forsakes God even under the deliverer, the providence, the unfaithfulness, the LORD's pity for their groanings to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And yet they would not hearken unto their judges, but they went a whoring after other gods, and bowed themselves unto them: they turned quickly out of the way which their fathers walked in, obeying the commandments of the LORD; but they did not so
that Israel would not hearken to their judges but went whoring after other gods
Israel refusing to obey the judges and turning again to idols, quickly forsaking the way of their godly fathers
the spiritual adultery that forsakes God even under the deliverer (Exodus 34:15)

schools.summary: That the LORD was with the judge and delivered Israel, moved by pity at their groanings: 'and when the LORD raised them up judges, then the LORD was with the judge, and delivered them out of the hand of their enemies all the days of the judge: for it repented the LORD because of their groanings by reason of them that oppressed them and vexed them' — the divine pity — 'and when the LORD raised them up judges, then the LORD was with the judge, and delivered them out of the hand of their enemies all the days of the judge: for it repented the LORD because of their groanings by reason of them that oppressed them and vexed them': God was with the judge to deliver, moved by pity at the people's groaning under oppression (the witnesses noting the significance — the tender compassion of God, who, though justly angry, was moved by the misery and groaning of his people, not because they deserved relief but because his mercy could not bear their distress, the 'repenting' not a change in God but the language of his pity, his unchanging mercy turning from judgment to deliverance, the LORD with the judge to give the victory, the groaning of the oppressed moving his compassion, 'in all their affliction he was afflicted,' Isaiah 63:9, the lesson — that God is moved by the misery of his people and hears their groaning, his compassion reaching to the undeserving in their distress, 'the LORD is very pitiful, and of tender mercy,' James 5:11, the truth that the 'repenting' of God is the language of accommodation, describing his real pity in human terms, his purpose unchanged but his mercy moved, 'I am God, and not man... I will not execute the fierceness of mine anger,' Hosea 11:9, the figure — the compassion of God in Christ, who is touched with the feeling of our infirmities and moved by the groaning of his people, 'like as a father pitieth his children, so the LORD pitieth them that fear him,' Psalm 103:13, the truth that the deliverance flows from God's pity and his presence with the deliverer, the figure of the tender compassion of God, moved by the misery of his people, the call to trust the pity of God in our distress, the assurance that God hears the groaning of his afflicted people, the figure of the tender compassion of God, who is moved by the misery of his people though they have sinned); the LORD being with the judge to deliver Israel all his days, the victory God's presence with the deliverer (the deliverance by God's presence, Isaiah 63:9) — the presence (the LORD with the judge); 'for it repented the LORD because of their groanings by reason of them that oppressed them and vexed them' (God moved by the groaning of his oppressed people, the 'repenting' the language of his real pity, his unchanging mercy turning to deliverance, the compassion that hears the afflicted, the tender mercy, James 5:11; Hosea 11:9) — the figure (the compassion of God in Christ, moved by the groaning of his people, the providence, the pity, the relapse when the judge died to follow, vv.19) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'it repented the LORD because of their groanings' signify?

sides.position: The tender compassion of God, moved by the misery of his people though they have sinned - God moved by their groaning not for their deserving but because his mercy could not bear their distress, the 'repenting' the language of his real pity and not a change in him (Isaiah 63:9; Hosea 11:9), his unchanging mercy turning from judgment to deliverance, the compassion of a father for his children (James 5:11; Psalm 103:13)

themes: And when the LORD raised them up judges, then the LORD was with the judge, and delivered them out of the hand of their enemies all the days of the judge: for it repented the LORD because of their groanings by reason of them that oppressed them and vexed them
that the LORD was with the judge and delivered Israel, moved by pity at their groanings (the crux of the tender compassion of God, moved by the misery of his people)

the tender compassion of God, who though justly angry was moved by the misery of his people, not for their deserving but because his mercy could not bear their distress, the 'repenting' the language of his pity not a change in him
his unchanging mercy turning from judgment to deliverance at the groaning of the oppressed, the compassion of a father for his children (Isaiah 63:9; James 5:11; Hosea 11:9; Psalm 103:13)

schools.summary: That when the judge died, Israel relapsed and corrupted themselves worse than their fathers: 'and it came to pass, when the judge was dead, that they returned, and corrupted themselves more than their fathers, in following other gods to serve them, and to bow down unto them; they ceased not from their own doings, nor from their stubborn way' — the deepening corruption — 'and it came to pass, when the judge was dead, that they returned, and corrupted themselves more than their fathers, in following other gods to serve them, and to bow down unto them; they ceased not from their own doings, nor from their stubborn way': as soon as the judge died, Israel relapsed into idolatry worse than before (the witnesses noting the significance — the obstinate and deepening corruption of a people unchanged by deliverance, returning to their idols the moment the restraint of the judge was removed, and sinking each time lower than the last, the heart unrenewed by mercy and unbroken by chastisement, the relapse worse than their fathers, the deliverance forgotten, the stubborn way unforsaken, the corruption deepening with each cycle, the lesson — that mercies and judgments alike fail to change the unrenewed heart, which returns to its sin the moment restraint is lifted, sinking deeper each time, 'as a dog returneth to his vomit, so a fool returneth to his folly,' Proverbs 26:11, the truth that the natural heart is stubborn and unchanged by outward deliverance, needing the inward renewal that only grace can give, 'can the Ethiopian change his skin, or the leopard his spots? then may ye also do good, that are accustomed to do evil,' Jeremiah 13:23, the figure — the apostate who, washed and returning, is worse than before, 'the last state of that man is worse than the first,' Matthew 12:45, the truth that without a renewed heart, deliverance only deepens the guilt of relapse, the figure of the deepening sin of a people unchanged by mercy, the obstinacy that returns to evil, the call to seek the inward renewal that grace alone gives, the warning that the heart unchanged by deliverance sinks deeper into sin, the figure of the deepening sin of a people unchanged by deliverance, the obstinacy that returns to evil); Israel relapsing the moment the judge died, corrupting themselves worse than their fathers in idolatry, the deliverance forgotten (the deepening corruption, Proverbs 26:11) — the relapse (worse than their fathers); 'they ceased not from their own doings, nor from their stubborn way' (Israel ceasing not from their evil doings and stubborn way, the heart unchanged by mercy and chastisement, needing the inward renewal grace alone gives, the unrenewed heart, Jeremiah 13:23; Matthew 12:45) — the figure (the apostate who returning is worse than before, the providence, the relapse, God's resolve to leave the nations to follow, vv.20-21) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Israel's relapse worse than their fathers signify?

sides.position: The deepening sin of a people unchanged by deliverance, the obstinacy that returns to evil - the obstinate corruption returning to idols the moment restraint was removed and sinking lower each cycle, the heart unrenewed by mercy, the dog returning to its vomit (Proverbs 26:11), the natural heart unchanged by outward deliverance and needing inward renewal (Jeremiah 13:23), the washed apostate worse than before (Matthew 12:45)

themes: And it came to pass, when the judge was dead, that they returned, and corrupted themselves more than their fathers, in following other gods to serve them, and to bow down unto them; they ceased not from their own doings, nor from their stubborn way
that when the judge died, Israel relapsed and corrupted themselves worse than their fathers (the crux of the deepening sin of a people unchanged by deliverance)
the obstinate and deepening corruption of a people unchanged by deliverance, returning to their idols the moment the restraint was removed and sinking lower each cycle, the heart unrenewed by mercy
the natural heart unchanged by outward deliverance and needing inward renewal, the washed apostate worse than before (Proverbs 26:11; Jeremiah 13:23; Matthew 12:45)

schools.summary: God's anger and the charge of covenant-breaking: 'and the anger of the LORD was hot against Israel; and he said, Because that this people hath transgressed my covenant which I commanded their fathers, and have not hearkened unto my voice' — God's anger burned because they had broken the covenant (the and the anger of the LORD was hot against Israel, God's anger, the and he said, Because that this people hath transgressed my covenant which I commanded their fathers, and have not hearkened unto my voice, the charge of covenant-breaking, the figure, God's anger at the broken covenant, the ground of the judgment, the truth that the breach of covenant and disobedience kindled God's anger, the just charge, Deuteronomy 31:16, the figure of the covenant broken provoking God's righteous anger, the anger of the LORD, the charge of covenant-breaking, vv.19); God's anger at the broken covenant (the judgment, Deuteronomy 31:16) — the anger (hot against Israel); 'this people hath transgressed my covenant... and have not hearkened unto my voice' (the charge of covenant-breaking, the ground of judgment, the figure, the covenant broken provoking God's anger, the providence, the charge, God's resolve to leave the nations to follow, vv.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the anger of the LORD was hot against Israel; and he said, Because that this people hath transgressed my covenant which I commanded their fathers, and have not hearkened unto my voice
God's anger and the charge of covenant-breaking
God's anger burning because Israel had broken the covenant and disobeyed his voice
the just ground of the judgment (Deuteronomy 31:16)

schools.summary: God's resolve to leave the nations Joshua had left: 'I also will not henceforth drive out any from before them of the nations which Joshua left when he died' — God resolved to leave the nations remaining at Joshua's death (the I also will not henceforth drive out any from before them of the nations which Joshua left when he died, the nations left, the figure, God leaving the nations as the just consequence of Israel's sin, the foe remaining, the truth that the nations Joshua had not driven out would be left as a scourge and a test, Judges 2:3, the figure of the foe left in the land by God's just judgment, the consequence of disobedience, the resolve to leave the nations, the foe remaining, vv.3); God resolving not to drive out the nations (the judgment, Judges 2:3) — the resolve (not drive out); 'the nations which Joshua left when he died' (the nations remaining at Joshua's death, the foe left, the figure, the foe left by God's just judgment, the providence, the resolve, the nations left to prove Israel to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: I also will not henceforth drive out any from before them of the nations which Joshua left when he died
God's resolve to leave the nations Joshua had left
God resolving to leave the nations remaining at Joshua's death
the foe left as the just consequence of Israel's sin (Judges 2:3)

schools.summary: That the nations were left to prove Israel: 'that through them I may prove Israel, whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not' — God left the nations to test whether Israel would keep his way (the that through them I may prove Israel, to prove Israel, the whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not, whether they would keep his way, the figure, the nations left as a test of Israel's faithfulness, the trial of obedience, the truth that God left the foe to prove whether Israel would walk in his way, the test of the heart, Deuteronomy 8:2, the figure of the trial that proves the heart, the foe a test of faithfulness, the proving of Israel, the test of obedience, vv.21); God proving Israel by the nations (the test, Deuteronomy 8:2) — the proving (through them); 'whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not' (the test whether Israel would keep God's way, the trial, the figure, the trial that proves the heart, the providence, the test, the nations left and the book's narrative to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: That through them I may prove Israel, whether they will keep the way of the LORD to walk therein, as their fathers did keep it, or not
that the nations were left to prove Israel
God leaving the nations to test whether Israel would keep his way
the trial that proves the heart's faithfulness (Deuteronomy 8:2)

schools.summary: The LORD leaving the nations, not driving them out hastily: 'therefore the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily; neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua' — God left the nations, not driving them out quickly, to be a test and scourge (the therefore the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily, the nations left, the neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua, not given to Joshua, the figure, God leaving the nations as a test, the foe remaining by his purpose, the truth that the nations were left, not given to Joshua, by God's design to prove Israel, Judges 3:1, the figure of the foe left in the land by God's purpose, the test of the people, the nations left, the close of the summary, vv.21-22); God leaving the nations as a test (the purpose, Judges 3:1) — the nations (left); 'neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua' (the nations not given to Joshua, by God's design, the purpose, the figure, the foe left by God's purpose, the providence, the close, the narrative of the judges to follow, ch.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Therefore the LORD left those nations, without driving them out hastily; neither delivered he them into the hand of Joshua
the LORD leaving the nations, not driving them out hastily
God leaving the nations, not driving them out quickly, to be the standing test and scourge of Israel by his design
the foe left in the land by God's purpose (Judges 3:1)
